event: Immaculate Conception of the Virgin Mary
symbols: white and blue robes — purity (white) and heavenly grace (blue), the traditional Marian colors
hands clasped at breast — receptive prayer and humble acceptance of God's singular grace
golden aureole — the radiance of divine favor surrounding the one full of grace
cherubs bearing flowers — angelic celebration of Mary's preservation from sin
lilies — virginal purity, recalling the lily of the Annunciation
roses — Mary as the mystical rose, beauty without the thorn of original sin
clouds beneath her feet — Mary elevated above the earthly realm, standing in the heavenly sphere
crescent form beneath feet — the woman clothed with the sun with the moon under her feet (Revelation 12:1)
description: A young woman stands upon billowing clouds, her body slightly turned, wearing flowing white and deep blue robes. Her hands are pressed together at her breast in prayer. A golden radiance surrounds her head and upper body. She gazes downward with a serene expression. Below her, several plump cherubs support her on the clouds; one carries pink roses and white lilies. Small winged cherub heads peek out from the clouds at the upper corners. The sky behind is a luminous pale blue.
theological_description: The Immaculate Conception — the doctrine that Mary was preserved from original sin from the moment of her conception — finds its visual expression in the iconographic type established by Pacheco and codified in Counter-Reformation art. The scriptural foundation draws from Genesis 3:15, where God declares enmity between the woman and the serpent: the Fathers (Irenaeus, Ephrem the Syrian, and later Duns Scotus) saw in this the promise that one woman would be entirely untouched by the serpent's poison. Revelation 12:1 provides the apocalyptic vision of 'a woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet and a crown of twelve stars,' which the tradition identifies with Mary in her immaculate glory. Luke 1:28 — 'Hail, full of grace' (kecharitomene) — was read by the Fathers as indicating a fullness of grace from the very beginning, not merely at the Annunciation. The lilies signify the purity proclaimed in the Song of Songs 2:2: 'As a lily among thorns, so is my love among the daughters.' The cherubs attending Mary recall that she is the new Ark of the Covenant (Luke 1:35, 2 Samuel 6:2), overshadowed by the divine presence as the cherubim overshadowed the mercy seat.

scripture_refs.reason: The woman clothed with the sun, moon under her feet — primary iconographic source for the Immaculate Conception image
Hail, full of grace — the angelic salutation indicating Mary's unique fullness of divine favor
Enmity between the woman and the serpent — the protoevangelium, Mary as the woman who crushes the serpent
The Holy Spirit will overshadow you — Mary as the new Ark overshadowed by divine presence
As a lily among thorns — Mary's purity among fallen humanity, reflected in the lilies carried by cherubs
Chosen before the foundation of the world to be holy and blameless — applied to Mary's predestined preservation
The king's daughter is all glorious within — the interior beauty and glory of the Immaculate Virgin
